elements.sent-2411-007: The problem of estimating the mean of a high-dimensional distribution given samples from the distribution is a foundational problem in statistics.
elements.sent-2411-008: In the problem of high-dimensional robust mean estimation, we are instead interested in estimating the mean of a distribution when we observe corrupted samples, making it a natural extension of mean estimation.
elements.sent-2411-009: The main challenge in high-dimensional robust mean estimation stems from the fact that efficient estimators that work well in one-dimension often do not generalize well to high dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-010: In particular, the error achieved by estimators such as the geometric median and coordinate-wise median scales with the dimension \cite{diakonikolas2023algorithmic while information-theoretically this dependence on dimension is unnecessary.}
elements.sent-2411-011: On the other hand estimators that achieved dimension-independent error such as the Tukey Median \cite{tukey1975mathematics were hard to compute \cite{johnson1978densest .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: The breakthrough works of \cite{diakonikolas2019robust, lai2016agnostic designed the first efficient algorithms for high-dimensional robust mean estimation for Gaussians that achieved near-optimal error.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Since then, there has been a plethora of work that has focused on different aspects on the problem of high-dimensional robust mean estimation under different distributional assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-014: Some of these aspects include designing faster and more practical algorithms \cite{diakonikolas2017being, cheng2019high , designing algorithms for specific corruption models \cite{diakonikolas2018robustly and exploiting structural properties such as sparsity \cite{balakrishnan2017computationally .}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Much of the work in robust mean estimation is in the following setting: The algorithm receives as input a corrupted set of samples, a corruption parameter $\varepsilon$, and other information about the underlying distribution.
elements.sent-2411-016: Typically, an $\varepsilon$ fraction of the samples is corrupted by an adversary with knowledge about the underlying distribution and consequently the true mean.
elements.sent-2411-017: The key distinction between different corruption models arises in how these $\varepsilon$ fraction of the samples are corrupted.
elements.sent-2411-018: In the strong contamination model an adversary has full ability to add or remove an $\varepsilon$ fraction of the samples, whereas in the additive contamination model, the adversary is only allowed to add corrupted samples and not remove uncorrupted samples.
elements.sent-2411-019: This distinction is critical as different models of corruption can lead to intriguing statistical-computational trade-offs \cite{diakonikolas2017statistical .}
elements.sent-2411-020: In this paper, we consider the strong contamination model and define an $\varepsilon$-corruption as follows.
elements.sent-2411-022: An adversary with unbounded computational power, knowledge of $\D$, $\mu^*$, and the algorithm inspects the samples $\uip$ and arbitrarily modifies at most an $\varepsilon$ fraction of the samples.
elements.sent-2411-023: The algorithm receives $\ip$ with the promise that at least $n \cdot (1-\varepsilon)$ samples of $\ip$ agrees with $\uip$.
elements.sent-2411-024: The corruption fraction $\varepsilon$ is also directly related to the breakdown point of the estimator.
elements.sent-2411-025: Informally, the breakdown point of an estimator is the maximum $\varepsilon$ that an estimator tolerates before the estimation error becomes unbounded.
elements.sent-2411-026: We note that the type of estimators that we consider have maximum breakdown point of $\frac{1 {2}$ \cite{rousseeuw2005robust, zhu2022robust .}}
elements.sent-2411-027: The maximum breakdown is critical as it marks the threshold of statistical identifiability: If $\varepsilon$ is $\frac{1 {2}$, an adversary can simulate the corrupted samples to appear from another distribution from the same family as the true underlying distribution.}

elements.sent-2411-029: Efficient estimators for robust mean estimation typically work in the regime where $\varepsilon$ is a sufficiently small constant bounded away from $\frac{1 {2}$.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Many of these algorithms achieve optimal/near-optimal error up to constants when $\varepsilon$ is sufficiently small \cite{diakonikolas2019robust, diakonikolas2017being,cheng2019high .}

elements.sent-2411-032: Two key methods have emerged as the workhorse behind significant recent developments in robust mean estimation: the filter approach \cite{diakonikolas2017being, diakonikolas2019robust that iteratively removes and down-weighs outliers, and the Sum-of-Squares (SoS) approach \cite{kothari2018robust,hopkins2018mixture , based on polynomial optimization.}}

elements.subsection-2411-020: Sample Complexity Dependence on Corruption Rate
elements.sent-2411-410: It is common practice in robust mean estimation papers to report the sample complexity dependence as a polynomial in $d$ and $\varepsilon^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-411: The polynomial in $\varepsilon^{-1}$ is taken in such a way that the sampling error and robust error are the same up to constant factors \footnote{The robust error is the error term that depends only on $\varepsilon$.}
elements.sent-2411-412: The other term is sampling error.
elements.sent-2411-413: This lends itself to the interpretation that in order to achieve vanishing estimation error, the number of samples required tends to infinity (since $\varepsilon^{-1 \to \infty$ as $\varepsilon \to 0$).}
elements.sent-2411-414: Throughout this paper we instead explicitly state both the robust and the sampling error terms and state sample complexity to reflect the number of samples required for our distributional assumptions to hold to the uniform distribution over the uncorrupted samples.
